summary: Steve Yegge argues that Joel Spolsky's famous 'Smart, and Gets Things Done' hiring heuristic is fundamentally flawed due to the Dunning-Kruger Effect—we can't reliably identify people smarter than ourselves in interviews. He proposes an alternative: 'Done, and Gets Things Smart,' meaning engineers who finish things impossibly fast AND improve systems while working on them. Drawing on his experiences at Geoworks, Amazon, and Google, Yegge emphasizes that successful tech companies need 'seed engineers'—freakishly brilliant people who establish engineering culture and can only be identified through extended work experience or reputation networks, not traditional interviews.
key_points: The Dunning-Kruger Effect makes it impossible to reliably identify candidates smarter than yourself in traditional interviews—you'll just hire clones of yourself
Having a good memory can actually impede understanding, letting you 'cheat your way through life' without developing real competence
Successful tech companies (Geoworks, Amazon, Google) all had brilliant 'seed engineers' who established lasting engineering culture
The only reliable ways to find exceptional engineers are working alongside them long enough to recognize their superiority, or asking around through reputation networks
'Done, and Gets Things Smart' engineers don't just complete tasks—they improve existing infrastructure and make systems smarter while doing their work
money_quotes: Having a good memory is a serious impediment to understanding. It lets you cheat your way through life.

It took me fifteen frigging years before I realized that there might in fact still be 'one or two things' left to learn, at which point I started looking outward and finally discovered how absolutely bambi-esquely thumperly incompetently clueless I really am.

How do you hire someone who's smarter than you? How do you tell if someone's smarter than you? This is a problem I've thought about, over nearly twenty years of interviewing, and it appears that the answer is: you can't. You just have to get lucky.
Interviews are conducted under pretty artificial conditions, and as a result they wind up being most effective at hiring people who are good at interviewing.
You can't do what Joel is asking you to do. You're not qualified. The Smart and Gets Things Done approach to interviewing will only get you copies of yourself.
For your startup, you don't want someone who's 'smart'. You want someone who's superhumanly godlike. Someone who can teach you a bunch of stuff.
These people aren't just pure gold; they're golden-egg-laying geese.
key_insight: Traditional interviews fail because the Dunning-Kruger Effect prevents us from recognizing people smarter than ourselves—the only way to find exceptional 'seed engineers' is through extended collaboration or reputation networks.
